title: Pride and Prejudice
author: Jane Austen
overture: Pride and Prejudice keeps to a few families in a country village — until it doesn't: a winter remove to London, an Easter in Kent, Lydia loose in Brighton, and a northern tour that ends on Mr Darcy's own lawn. Austen sanctioned no map and named her distances instead, so the conjectured pins here run on her arithmetic: one mile to Meryton, twenty-four to Gracechurch Street, five from Lambton to Pemberley.

sources.name: Kenneth Smith, 'The Probable Location of Longbourn' (Persuasions 27, 2005)
Janine Barchas, 'How Celebrity Name-Dropping Leads to Another Model for Pemberley' (Persuasions 35, 2013)

characters.name: Elizabeth Bennet
characters.role: The second Bennet daughter — quick, satirical, and the best walker in the book; her longest journeys, into Kent and up to Derbyshire, are the ones that change her mind

characters.name: Fitzwilliam Darcy
characters.role: Master of Pemberley, ten thousand a year; proud at the Meryton assembly, humbled in Derbyshire — his most important journey is made in secret and explained late

characters.name: Jane Bennet
characters.role: The eldest and the beauty, serene where her sister sparks; her one reckless journey — three miles on horseback into the rain — is her mother's doing

characters.name: Lydia Bennet
characters.role: The youngest, fifteen and untamed; from the elopement onward her track carries Wickham too — Brighton to Clapham to a patched-up wedding and a Newcastle posting

locations.name: West of Hertford, Hertfordshire
locations.note: No original exists; the pin is fixed purely by Austen's stated distances — one mile from Meryton, twenty-four from Gracechurch Street — a mile west of the Hertford conjecture. Kenneth Smith (Persuasions 27, 2005) ran the same triangulation and came out near Harpenden instead; the method is agreed even where the answer isn't.
locations.story: The Bennet home: an entailed estate of two thousand a year, five unmarried daughters and a father barricaded in his library. The novel opens here, closes here, and keeps coming back to catch its breath — and Mr Collins, who will inherit the place, proposes accordingly.
locations.quote: The village of Longbourn was only one mile from Meryton; a most convenient distance for the young ladies

image.caption: Colney House, Hertfordshire — a modest Georgian country seat of the Longbourn kind, engraved in the 1820s. Indicative of the neighbourhood; no original for Longbourn exists.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, engraved by T. Barber, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1825) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Hertford, Hertfordshire
locations.note: Austen sanctioned no identifications. Jamie Everitt ('Meryton Identified!', Persuasions On-Line 42.1, 2021) makes the case for Hertford: a county market town with a proper assembly room, off the main coach roads as Meryton must be, and sitting right for the twenty-four miles to Gracechurch Street. Ware and Harpenden are the older rivals.
locations.story: The market town a mile's walk away: aunt Philips, the milliner's shop, and — from October — the militia's winter quarters. Its assembly is where Darcy declines to be introduced to anybody and is settled to be the proudest, most disagreeable man in the world.
locations.quote: they were well supplied both with news and happiness by the recent arrival of a militia regiment in the neighbourhood; it was to remain the whole winter, and Meryton was the head-quarters

image.caption: A Hertfordshire village near Ware, drawn in 1814 — the country Meryton's little market town would have sat in. Indicative; Austen named no original.
image.credit: John Linnell, 'View of Amwell Hill, near Ware, in Hertfordshire' (1814) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Balls Park, Hertford
locations.note: Three miles from Longbourn is all Austen gives, and no candidate has ever stuck. Balls Park, south-east of Hertford, stands in here as the right kind of house in the right place under Everitt's geography.
locations.story: The house Bingley takes by Michaelmas, and the question that opens the novel. Jane's providential cold buys both elder sisters a five-day residence; the ball in late November knots up half the plot, and the party's abrupt remove to town unties none of it.
locations.quote: have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?

image.caption: Panshanger, Hertfordshire — a substantial county house in its park, of the kind Bingley takes at Netherfield, engraved in 1818. Indicative; no original for Netherfield has ever stuck.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, etched by J. Webb, engraved by J. Bishop, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1818) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Gracechurch Street, City of London
locations.note: Real, and precisely chosen: a merchant's address in the City, within view of Mr Gardiner's own warehouses, for Bingley's sisters to sneer at. When Darcy's unnamed town house is meant, this pin stands for 'town' generally.
locations.story: The Gardiners' comfortable house in the trading City — the novel's London base and its moral counterweight: the relations Darcy should blush for prove to be the ones he ends by loving. Jane winters here unhappily; the hunt for Wickham is run from here; Lydia is married from here.
locations.quote: Mr. Darcy may, perhaps, have heard of such a place as Gracechurch Street, but he would hardly think a month's ablution enough to cleanse him from its impurities

image.caption: Cheapside and Bow Church, in the trading City a few streets from Gracechurch Street, engraved 1837 — the merchant London of the Gardiners.
image.credit: Engraved by W. Albutt after Thomas H. Shepherd, published 1837 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Near Westerham, Kent
locations.note: 'Hunsford, near Westerham, Kent' is the dateline of Mr Collins's first letter — Austen's one anchor, and this pin's only warrant. Neither the parsonage nor Rosings has a settled original; Lady Catherine's park is folded into the same pin.
locations.story: Mr Collins's parsonage, with Rosings and its glazing across the lane. Elizabeth's six-week Easter visit collects the novel's hinge: Darcy's first proposal, delivered with more pride than love, and the letter next morning that starts the long revision of everything she thought she knew.
locations.quoteRef: Ch. 13, the dateline of Mr Collins's letter

image.caption: Godmersham Park, Kent — a great house in its wooded park, of the Rosings kind (and, as it happens, Austen's own brother's seat), engraved in the 1820s. Indicative; neither Rosings nor Hunsford has a settled original.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, engraved by T. Jeavons, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1826) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The Bell, Bromley
locations.note: Textual down to the name of the inn: a real coaching house on the Hastings road, and the appointed change of horses on the way out of Kent.
locations.story: Where Lady Catherine's condescension extends to route-planning: change horses at Bromley, mention her name at the Bell, and you will be attended to. Elizabeth and Maria duly pass this way out of Kent.
locations.quote: Where shall you change horses? Oh, Bromley, of course. If you mention my name at the Bell, you will be attended to.

locations.name: Brighton, Sussex
locations.story: The camp, the sea, and a whole campful of officers: everything Lydia's imagination requires. Mr Bennet lets her go as the cheaper way of teaching her her own insignificance — a calculation the third volume spends fifty pages correcting.
locations.quote: In Lydia's imagination, a visit to Brighton comprised every possibility of earthly happiness

image.caption: The Royal Pavilion, Brighton — the Regency resort in its heyday, from Nash's Views of the Royal Pavilion, 1826.
image.credit: From John Nash, Views of the Royal Pavilion, Brighton (1826) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Ramsgate, Kent
locations.story: Backstory, not itinerary — no track touches it. The summer before the novel, Wickham followed fifteen-year-old Georgiana Darcy here and talked her into an elopement her brother forestalled by a day or two. Darcy's letter discloses it; it is why Lydia's flight lands on him like a repetition.
locations.quote: last summer she went with the lady who presided over it to Ramsgate; and thither also went Mr. Wickham, undoubtedly by design

image.caption: Ramsgate town and harbour, the Kent watering-place, in an aquatint of 1799.
image.credit: James Ward, 'View of Ramsgate Town and Harbour' (1799) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Bakewell, Derbyshire
locations.note: Bakewell is the common identification — Austen is said to have stayed at the Rutland Arms there, and the town sits where the tour needs it; Old Brampton is the persistent rival. The pin follows the tradition without leaning on it.
locations.story: Mrs Gardiner's girlhood town, and the inn where the tour pauses — not five miles from Pemberley. Darcy and Georgiana call; the return visits multiply; and it is here, with Darcy in the room, that Jane's two letters arrive and the Lydia disaster breaks.
locations.quote: To the little town of Lambton, the scene of Mrs. Gardiner's former residence, and where she had lately learned that some acquaintance still remained, they bent their steps

image.caption: Dovedale, in the Derbyshire Peak — one of the 'celebrated beauties' Austen names, standing here for the wooded Peak country of Lambton. Indicative; Lambton is a conjectured place.
image.credit: Joseph Wright of Derby, 'Dovedale' (c.1786) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: North of Bakewell, Derbyshire
locations.note: The pin obeys Austen's only hard datum: five miles from Lambton. Donald Greene ('Pemberley Revisited', Persuasions 1) argued for Chatsworth — but the novel's tourists visit Chatsworth as itself among the celebrated beauties, so Pemberley can hardly textually be it. Janine Barchas (Persuasions 35) makes the richer case for Wentworth Woodhouse, seat of the real Fitzwilliams, as the house in Austen's mind, if not on her map.
locations.story: The house that turns the novel: the stream swelled without artificial appearance, the housekeeper's testimony, 'of this place I might have been mistress' — and the owner home a day early, walking up from the stables a changed man. The second half of the book starts on this lawn.
locations.quote: the eye was instantly caught by Pemberley House, situated on the opposite side of the valley, into which the road with some abruptness wound. It was a large, handsome stone building, standing well on rising ground

image.caption: Matlock Bath in the Derwent valley — the Derbyshire Peak country Pemberley is set among, with the river winding below the crags, painted in 1789. Indicative; Pemberley is a conjectured place.
image.credit: William Day, 'Matlock Baths, Derbyshire' (1789) · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Chatsworth House, Derbyshire

image.caption: Chatsworth House, Derbyshire, in an engraving of 1880
image.credit: F. O. Morris, Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen, 1880 · public domain

locations.note: Real, and visited as itself — which is the standing objection to Chatsworth-as-Pemberley, whatever the film adaptations have since decided.
locations.story: One of 'the celebrated beauties' the tourists take in on the road north — and the quiet joke of the Pemberley debate: Elizabeth sees Chatsworth as Chatsworth, days before Pemberley overwhelms her as Pemberley. The adaptations have been merrily conflating the two ever since.
locations.quote: as great an object of her curiosity as all the celebrated beauties of Matlock, Chatsworth, Dovedale, or the Peak

locations.name: Newcastle upon Tyne
locations.story: Wickham's commission in the regulars — Darcy's money, nobody's thanks — and the map's furthest, most grudging point: the Wickhams' marriage banished north to cool, further than even Mrs Bennet can conveniently visit.
locations.quote: They are gone down to Newcastle, a place quite northward it seems, and there they are to stay, I do not know how long

image.caption: Newcastle upon Tyne from across the river, the map's furthest, most grudging point, in a steel engraving of 1837.
image.credit: 'Newcastle-on-Tyne', steel engraving, 1837 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.note: Sent on horseback with rain coming on — 'then you must stay all night': Mrs Bennet's scheme answers beyond her hopes, and the violent cold that follows buys a five-day residence
The famous walk to Jane's sickbed: three miles, crossing field after field, jumping over stiles and springing over puddles — arriving with a glowing face and a petticoat six inches deep in mud
Home in Mr Bingley's chaise on the Sunday, against their mother's best obstruction

via.note: down out of Hertford to join the London road in the Lea valley

via.name: Waltham Cross
via.note: the Eleanor cross on the road south

via.name: Edmonton

movements.routeNote: Out of the Hertford country down the Lea valley — Hoddesdon, Waltham Cross, Edmonton and Tottenham — and in through Shoreditch to the City.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter.
movements.note: The whole Netherfield party decamps to town within days of the ball — Darcy's engineering, confessed much later. His own house is in a smarter quarter; Gracechurch Street stands in for 'town' on this map

via.note: down out of Hertford to join the London road in the Lea valley

movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter.
movements.note: To town with the Gardiners at the end of their Christmas visit, on a hope she barely admits; a fortnight of Miss Bingley's silence does the rest

via.name: Hoddesdon

movements.routeNote: The twenty-four miles Austen counts: out of the Hertford country down the Lea valley — Hoddesdon, Waltham Cross, Edmonton and Tottenham — and in through Shoreditch to the City.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter. Austen fixes the distance herself (ch. 27, 'in Gracechurch Street by noon').
movements.note: With Sir William Lucas and Maria — twenty-four miles, 'in Gracechurch Street by noon' — for a night with the Gardiners and an evening at the theatre

via.name: Bromley
via.note: out of London on the Kent road — Lady Catherine's appointed change of horses

via.name: Keston
via.note: up onto the Kent heath, bearing for Westerham

movements.routeNote: Out of the City over the river and down the Kent road through Bromley, then bearing off by Keston for Westerham and the parsonage at Hunsford.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Bromley–Westerham road into west Kent. Austen names Bromley as the change of horses (ch. 37).
movements.note: 'Every object in the next day's journey was new and interesting': into Kent to the parsonage gate, Mr Collins pointing out every prospect on the way

via.name: Bromley
via.note: out of London on the Kent road — Lady Catherine's appointed change of horses

via.name: Keston
via.note: up onto the Kent heath, bearing for Westerham

movements.routeNote: Out of London over the river and down the Kent road through Bromley, then bearing off by Keston for Westerham and Rosings.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Bromley–Westerham road into west Kent. Austen names Bromley as the change of horses (ch. 37).
movements.note: The Easter pilgrimage to Lady Catherine, bringing Colonel Fitzwilliam; the visitors at the parsonage complicate it fatally

via.name: Keston
via.note: off the Kent heath, coming down for Bromley

via.name: Bromley
via.note: the change of horses on the road back into London

movements.routeNote: Back off the Westerham road by Keston to Bromley, then up the Kent road and over the river into the City.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Bromley–Westerham road into west Kent. Austen names Bromley as the change of horses (ch. 37).
movements.note: The gentlemen leave Rosings the morning after the letter is put into Elizabeth's hand in the grove
Away with Maria after six weeks, the horses changed at Bromley according to Lady Catherine's instructions
On to the Gardiners' for a few days, the whole history of the proposal untold in her pocket

via.name: Tottenham
via.note: out of the City through Shoreditch onto the Lea valley road

via.name: Edmonton
via.note: up the Lea valley out of Middlesex

via.name: Waltham Cross
via.note: the Eleanor cross on the road north

via.name: Hoddesdon
via.note: turning off for Hertford and the Longbourn country

movements.routeNote: Home up the Lea valley — through Tottenham, Edmonton, Waltham Cross and Hoddesdon — and off the London road into the Hertford country.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter.
movements.note: 'The second week in May': home together, met at the George by Kitty and Lydia with a table of cold meat and the news that the regiment is off to Brighton

via.name: London
via.note: Down through the City and over the river.

via.name: Croydon
via.note: Onto the Brighton turnpike.

via.name: Cuckfield
via.note: The last posting stop before Brighton; the King's Head.

movements.routeNote: From Hertfordshire through London, then the Brighton Road turnpike via Croydon, Reigate, Crawley and Cuckfield.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads; the London-to-Brighton turnpike (Act of 1770). Charles G. Harper, The Brighton Road.
movements.note: With Mrs Forster, particular friend of two months' standing — off from Meryton early in the morning, Kitty weeping with envy, Lydia in raptures

via.name: Blenheim
via.note: the palace at Woodstock

via.name: Kenilworth
via.note: the ruined castle

via.name: Matlock
via.note: into the Peak, on toward Chatsworth

movements.routeNote: The Gardiners' tour, shrunk from the Lakes to Derbyshire, keeping to the road Austen names in a single sentence.
movements.routeSource: Austen names the route herself, ch. 42: 'Oxford, Blenheim, Warwick, Kenilworth, Birmingham, etc., are sufficiently known.'
movements.note: The tour, shrunk from the Lakes to Derbyshire; Austen does the whole road in a sentence — 'Oxford, Blenheim, Warwick, Kenilworth, Birmingham, etc., are sufficiently known' — and the celebrated beauties of Matlock and Chatsworth get their due
The principal wonders of the country seen, they bend their steps to the little town of Lambton — with Pemberley, Elizabeth learns from her aunt, not five miles off

via.name: St Albans
via.note: Out of London on the Holyhead road as far as Redbourn.

via.name: Matlock
via.note: Into the Peak, towards Bakewell and Chatsworth.

movements.routeNote: The London-to-Derby coaching road — St Albans, Dunstable, Northampton, Leicester, Loughborough and Derby, then up into the Peak by Matlock.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads; Cary's New Itinerary — the London (Swan with Two Necks) to Derby and Manchester road, 126 miles to Derby.
movements.note: Down from town a day before his housekeeper expects him — in time to walk into the tourists on his own ground
'To Pemberley, therefore, they were to go' — the family safely away, the chambermaid said; the eye instantly caught, the owner suddenly present
Back to the inn in a whirl: the housekeeper's praise, the portrait in the gallery, and Mr Darcy asking leave to introduce his sister
Returning Georgiana's call with Mrs Gardiner: the saloon, cold meat and the finest fruits of the season, under Miss Bingley's glare
Back to the inn again; the fishing party is fixed, and everything is going unaccountably well

via.name: Epsom
via.note: the first post north from Brighton, where the chaise was traced

via.name: Clapham
via.note: where they left the chaise for a hackney-coach and vanished into London

movements.routeNote: The elopement's own trace: up from Brighton to Epsom and on to Clapham, where the chaise was given up for a hackney-coach into London — the road the pursuers followed to the point the scent went cold.
movements.routeSource: Austen names the stages herself (ch. 47): traced 'to Epsom', then 'to Clapham', where 'they were seen to continue the London road'.
movements.note: The elopement: off with Wickham on the Sunday night, traced through Epsom to Clapham — where they moved into a hackney-coach and vanished into London. Scotland was never in his plan
Calling at the inn just as Jane's letters do their work; he stays while Elizabeth breaks down, and goes away grave
Back the same morning, having heard names — Wickham, Mrs Younge — that he can act on

via.name: Matlock
via.note: Into the Peak, towards Bakewell and Chatsworth.

via.name: St Albans
via.note: Out of London on the Holyhead road as far as Redbourn.

movements.routeNote: The London-to-Derby coaching road — St Albans, Dunstable, Northampton, Leicester, Loughborough and Derby, then up into the Peak by Matlock.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads; Cary's New Itinerary — the London (Swan with Two Necks) to Derby and Manchester road, 126 miles to Derby.
movements.note: Everything undone in half an hour: express home, false excuses left for the Lambton friends, one night on the road

via.name: Matlock
via.note: Into the Peak, towards Bakewell and Chatsworth.

via.name: St Albans
via.note: Out of London on the Holyhead road as far as Redbourn.

movements.routeNote: The London-to-Derby coaching road — St Albans, Dunstable, Northampton, Leicester, Loughborough and Derby, then up into the Peak by Matlock.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads; Cary's New Itinerary — the London (Swan with Two Necks) to Derby and Manchester road, 126 miles to Derby.
movements.note: The secret hunt, reported only in Mrs Gardiner's September letter: he left Derbyshire a day behind the travellers, found Mrs Younge, found Wickham, and bought the wedding

via.name: Tottenham
via.note: out of the City through Shoreditch onto the Lea valley road

via.name: Edmonton
via.note: up the Lea valley out of Middlesex

via.name: Waltham Cross
via.note: the Eleanor cross on the road north

via.name: Hoddesdon
via.note: turning off for Hertford and the Longbourn country

movements.routeNote: Up the Lea valley from the City — Tottenham, Edmonton, Waltham Cross and Hoddesdon — and off the London road into the Hertford country.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter.
movements.note: Married from the Gardiners' house at St Clement's and straight home to show the ring: 'Ah! Jane, I take your place now'

via.name: Ware
via.note: Join the Great North Road out of Hertfordshire.

via.name: Newark
via.note: Newark-on-Trent.

via.name: Retford
via.note: East Retford.

via.name: Ferrybridge
via.note: A thriving cluster of coaching inns.

via.name: Boroughbridge
via.note: The direct northern line, by-passing York.

movements.routeNote: The Great North Road north out of Hertfordshire — Ware, Royston, Huntingdon, Stamford, Grantham, Newark, Retford, Doncaster, Ferrybridge, then the direct Boroughbridge line through Northallerton, Darlington and Durham to Newcastle.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary (1798); Paterson's Roads — the London-to-Edinburgh Great North Road.
movements.note: North to Wickham's commission in the regulars — 'a place quite northward, it seems' — the marriage posted out of everybody's way

via.name: Tottenham
via.note: out of the City through Shoreditch onto the Lea valley road

via.name: Edmonton
via.note: up the Lea valley out of Middlesex

via.name: Waltham Cross
via.note: the Eleanor cross on the road north

via.name: Hoddesdon
via.note: turning off for Hertford and Netherfield

movements.routeNote: Up the Lea valley from the City — Tottenham, Edmonton, Waltham Cross and Hoddesdon — and off the London road into the Hertford country.
movements.routeSource: Paterson's Roads / Cary's New Itinerary — the London–Hertford–Cambridge road down the Lea valley, the standard coaching approach to the City from that quarter.
movements.note: Back with Bingley, nominally for the shooting; Mrs Bennet's civilities all go to the wrong man
The last three miles of the map: the walk out towards Lucas Lodge on which Elizabeth thanks him for Lydia — and the second proposal fares better than the first

story.title: Longbourn
story.narration: At Longbourn in Hertfordshire live the five Bennet sisters, whose estate is entailed away from them to a distant cousin. Elizabeth is the second — quick, satirical, her father's favourite. Her mother's whole business in life is getting the girls married.

story.title: The assembly
story.narration: A rich young man, Mr Bingley, takes the neighbouring house of Netherfield and brings a still richer friend. At the Meryton assembly Mr Darcy — ten thousand a year, and proud with it — calls Elizabeth merely tolerable. She overhears, and decides to dislike him.

story.title: Netherfield
story.narration: Netherfield is three miles off. Bingley falls at once for the eldest sister, gentle Jane; Darcy, against his own judgement, finds himself watching Elizabeth's dark eyes across every drawing room in the county.

story.title: Sent in the rain
story.narration: Mrs Bennet's scheming: she sends Jane to dine at Netherfield on horseback, with rain coming on, so she must stay the night. Jane stays — but catches a violent cold, and takes to a sickbed there instead.
Back at Longbourn next morning, the news of Jane's cold arrives, and no carriage can be spared. Elizabeth simply sets off on foot.

story.title: The walk
story.narration: Elizabeth walks the three miles to nurse her — crossing field after field, over stiles and puddles, arriving with a glowing face and a petticoat six inches deep in mud. The ladies of Netherfield are appalled; Darcy is not.

story.title: Under the same roof
story.narration: For five days Elizabeth and Darcy are shut up together while Jane mends. They spar over books and character; Miss Bingley angles for Darcy and needles Elizabeth; and Darcy, alarmingly, feels the danger of caring too much.

story.title: Home again
story.narration: Jane recovered, the sisters go home in Bingley's carriage on the Sunday, against their mother's every obstruction. Behind them Netherfield's welcome has cooled — Darcy relieved to see the back of a temptation he never meant to feel.

story.title: The militia, and Mr Wickham
story.narration: A militia regiment winters at Meryton, and with it charming Mr Wickham, who tells Elizabeth that Darcy cheated him of a living his own father meant him to have. She believes every word — it fits the proud man she already despises.

story.title: The party breaks up
story.narration: Leave Elizabeth a moment for Darcy. He has seen enough: Bingley is sliding towards a Bennet marriage he thinks beneath him. So he acts — quietly, and, he tells himself, kindly. The whole Netherfield party is to decamp to London within days of the ball.

story.title: To town
story.narration: They are gone. Jane is left with a cold note and no explanation, and Bingley — kept in town, kept in the dark — never comes back. Elizabeth suspects Darcy's hand in it, and is right, though it will be a year before she knows how right.

story.title: Jane goes to London
story.narration: At Christmas the Gardiners — Mrs Bennet's sensible London relations — carry Jane back with them to Gracechurch Street, on a hope she will barely name: that she might see Bingley. A fortnight of Miss Bingley's cool silence answers it.

story.title: Twenty-four miles
story.narration: In March Elizabeth travels up with Sir William Lucas — twenty-four miles, in Gracechurch Street by noon — for a night with the Gardiners and the theatre, before going on into Kent. Her friend Charlotte has married the odious Mr Collins, and asked her to visit.

story.title: Into Kent
story.narration: Every object of the day's journey is new: down into Kent to the parsonage gate, Mr Collins pointing out each prospect. He is clerk to the great Lady Catherine de Bourgh, whose house, Rosings, looms over his garden — and whose nephew is Mr Darcy.

story.title: Rosings
story.narration: At Hunsford, Elizabeth is paraded before Lady Catherine — imperious, prying, certain of her own importance, dispensing opinions on everything from music to the carving of a joint. Charlotte, at least, has made a bearable life of it, between the parsonage and the great house.

story.title: The nephew comes down
story.narration: And down to Rosings, for his yearly Easter visit to his aunt, comes Darcy himself — bringing his cousin, the easy Colonel Fitzwilliam. He had not known Elizabeth would be there. Now that she is, he cannot keep away from the parsonage.

story.title: The Easter visit
story.narration: He calls, and calls again, and says almost nothing; Elizabeth thinks him merely proud. Then Colonel Fitzwilliam lets slip that Darcy has lately congratulated himself on saving a friend from an imprudent marriage. Elizabeth knows exactly whose marriage he means.

story.title: The first proposal
story.narration: That evening Darcy comes to the parsonage and, to her astonishment, proposes — ardently, and insultingly, dwelling on her low connections and his own struggle against her. Elizabeth refuses him flatly: for Jane, for Wickham, and for the last man in the world she could be prevailed on to marry.

story.title: The letter
story.narration: Next morning he puts a letter into her hand in the grove and walks away. It answers everything: yes, he divided Bingley from Jane, thinking her indifferent — but Wickham is a liar, who once tried to elope with Darcy's young sister for her fortune. Elizabeth reads it until she is ashamed to the bone.

story.title: He is gone
story.narration: The gentlemen leave Rosings the very next morning, back towards town, the letter already doing its slow work. Elizabeth stays on another fortnight, her whole picture of two men — the charming one and the proud one — quietly turning inside out.

story.title: Leaving Kent
story.narration: After six weeks Elizabeth sets off home with Charlotte's sister, the horses changed at the Bell in Bromley exactly as Lady Catherine instructed. She carries the unread history of the proposal in her pocket, and cannot decide whom to tell.

story.title: Back to the Gardiners
story.narration: On to Gracechurch Street, where Jane has waited all winter. Elizabeth tells her the half she can — Wickham's true character — but keeps back the proposal, and its worse revelation: that Darcy was the hand behind Bingley's silence.

story.title: The sisters come home
story.narration: The second week in May the sisters travel home together, met at the George by Kitty and Lydia with cold meat and a torrent of news — chief of it that the regiment is leaving for Brighton, and taking the family's peace with it.

story.title: Lydia's turn
story.narration: Now the youngest sister. Lydia is fifteen, loud and man-mad, and the colonel's flighty wife has invited her to Brighton. Elizabeth begs her father to forbid it — a garrison town is no place for Lydia — but he cannot be bothered to withstand the noise.

story.title: Off to Brighton
story.narration: So off she goes at daybreak, Kitty weeping with envy and Lydia in raptures, to a summer of officers by the sea. Wickham's regiment is quartered there — and Wickham, his Meryton debts mounting, is glad enough of the change.
Meanwhile Elizabeth sets out on the pleasure she has been promised all year: a summer tour with the Gardiners, shrunk from the Lakes to the nearer beauties of Derbyshire.

story.title: The Derbyshire tour
story.narration: Austen does the whole road in a sentence — Oxford, Blenheim, Warwick, Kenilworth, Birmingham, sufficiently known — and lands them among the peaks. They see the celebrated wonders of the county, Matlock and the great house at Chatsworth, working north.

story.title: Lambton
story.narration: The principal sights seen, they bend towards the little town of Lambton, where Mrs Gardiner spent her girlhood. And there Elizabeth learns the one fact she would have chosen to avoid: Pemberley, Mr Darcy's seat, lies not five miles off.
And Darcy himself, unknown to her, is already on the road north — down from London a day ahead of his own household, straight towards the ground she is about to walk onto.

story.title: Home before he is expected
story.narration: He arrives at Pemberley a day before his housekeeper looks for him — in time, though neither of them knows it yet, to walk out of his own woods and straight into a party of tourists on his lawn.
For at Lambton, that same morning, Elizabeth has been talked into the one visit she meant to avoid. The two threads are about to cross on his own lawn.

story.title: To Pemberley
story.narration: Assured by the chambermaid that the family is away, Elizabeth agrees to see the house. Pemberley takes her breath — grounds without a hint of artifice, a housekeeper who calls her master the best of men — and then, on the lawn, the master himself, home a day early.

story.title: Back to the inn
story.narration: It is agony and wonder both. This Darcy is courteous, almost warm — asks after her family, walks with her uncle, begs leave to introduce his sister. Elizabeth returns to Lambton in a whirl, no longer sure of a single thing she thought she knew.

story.title: Georgiana's welcome
story.narration: Two days on, Elizabeth and Mrs Gardiner return to Pemberley to meet shy young Georgiana Darcy — cold meat, the finest fruits of the season, and Miss Bingley's jealous glare. Everything, unaccountably, is going well; a fishing party is even fixed for the gentlemen.

story.title: The happiest days
story.narration: Back to the inn again, lighter than she has been in months. She half-admits to herself what she once thought impossible — that she could, in time, have loved him. And then, next morning, two letters from Jane arrive at once.

story.title: What the letters carry
story.narration: The letters are about Lydia. Wind back to Brighton: on the Sunday night she has run off with Wickham — for Scotland and a wedding, everyone hopes, but Scotland was never his plan.

story.title: Lost in London
story.narration: They are traced through Epsom to Clapham, where the pair left their chaise for a hackney-coach and vanished into London — unmarried, unfindable, and ruining every Bennet sister's prospects with them. A girl of fifteen, gone with a man who means never to marry her.
Now the two threads meet. Wind back to the Lambton inn, on the very morning the letters come: Darcy calls on Elizabeth, and walks in on her in tears, the ruin of her family fresh in her hand.

story.title: He finds her weeping
story.narration: She tells him everything — the elopement, the certainty of disgrace — and he grows grave and quiet and soon takes his leave. Elizabeth is sure the scandal has killed whatever he felt, and that she has lost him at the very moment she wanted him.

story.title: A name he can use
story.narration: But Darcy has heard two names he can act on — Wickham, and Mrs Younge, the woman who once helped him prey on Georgiana. He rides back to Pemberley with a plan already forming, and quietly leaves Derbyshire a day behind the runaways.
Wind back to that same morning at the inn, and to Elizabeth, left with the tour in ruins around her. There is nothing to do but go home.

story.title: Express home
story.narration: Everything undone in half an hour: the tour abandoned, false excuses left for the Lambton friends, one night on the road. Elizabeth reaches Longbourn to a house in uproar — her mother prostrate, her father gone to London to hunt for the runaways.
And while Longbourn waits and Mr Bennet searches in vain, a second search is going on that no one at home knows anything about.

story.title: The secret hunt
story.narration: Darcy has followed the runaways to town a day behind them. He runs Mrs Younge to ground, finds Wickham, and — for Elizabeth's sake, telling no one — pays his debts and buys the wedding that saves her sister's name. It will surface only later, in a letter.

story.title: The ring
story.narration: Married at last from the Gardiners' house, Lydia comes home to flaunt the wedding ring — brazen, delighted, wholly unrepentant. 'Ah, Jane, I take your place now,' she tells her eldest sister, and lets slip that Mr Darcy, of all people, was at the church.

story.title: What Darcy did
story.narration: That name, unaccountable at a Wickham wedding, sends Elizabeth to her aunt for the truth. Mrs Gardiner's letter tells all: it was Darcy who found them, Darcy who paid, Darcy who arranged everything, and swore the Gardiners to silence. He did it, Elizabeth understands, for her.

story.title: Lydia sent north
story.narration: Lydia and her Wickham do not linger. His reward for marrying her is a commission in the regulars, and the newlyweds are soon packed off to make their careless way in the world.

story.title: Northward, out of the way
story.narration: North to Newcastle — a place quite northward, it seems — the marriage and its shame posted well out of everybody's way. Lydia goes as merrily as she does everything, leaving her sisters to gather up the family's ruined credit behind her.
And back in Hertfordshire, at the very time Lydia is setting off, the two men who left it a year ago are riding towards it again.

story.title: The right man returns
story.narration: Bingley reopens Netherfield for the shooting, and Darcy comes with him, drawn by no bird in Hertfordshire. Freed at last of Darcy's interference, Bingley is soon on his knees to Jane — one sister happily settled, while Elizabeth watches Darcy and dares not hope for herself.

story.title: Lady Catherine's raid
story.narration: Then Lady Catherine descends on Longbourn in a fury, having heard a rumour that Elizabeth means to marry her nephew, and demands she promise never to accept him. Elizabeth, magnificently, refuses to promise anything at all — and sends the great lady off defeated.

story.title: The second proposal
story.narration: It is the last three miles of the whole map. Lady Catherine's report of Elizabeth's defiance has given Darcy a thread of hope, and he walks over towards Lucas Lodge to test it. Elizabeth thanks him for Lydia; he asks again; and this time the answer is yes.

story.title: Both threads home
story.narration: Two weddings close the book. Jane has her Bingley and Elizabeth her Darcy, and the pair of them go north to Pemberley — the proud man and the prejudiced woman having taught each other, over field and parsonage and great house, how wrong a first glance can be.
